quiz_title: AL — Conditioning & Fitness - Physical Conditioning Fundamentals [EN]

questions.text: What does the term 'aerobic fitness' refer to in football?
questions.explanation: Aerobic fitness refers to the ability of the cardiovascular and respiratory systems to supply oxygen to working muscles over a sustained period, underpinning a player's endurance throughout a 90-minute match.

options.text: The ability to sustain effort over a long period using oxygen
Short explosive bursts of maximum speed
Flexibility and joint range of motion
Upper body strength for aerial challenges

questions.text: Why is warming up important before a football training session?
questions.explanation: A warm-up gradually increases heart rate, blood flow to muscles, and core temperature, reducing injury risk and preparing the neuromuscular system for more intense activity.

options.text: To increase blood flow, raise muscle temperature, and reduce injury risk
To burn as many calories as possible before training
To stretch as far as possible before any movement
To give players time to socialise before the session begins

questions.text: What is the recommended minimum daily water intake for an active football player during training?
questions.explanation: Active athletes typically need 2–3 litres or more per day depending on sweat loss. Adequate hydration maintains performance and prevents cramps and fatigue.

options.text: At least 2–3 litres, increasing with sweat loss
0.5 litres — excess water causes cramps
1 litre — the standard for all adults
5 litres exactly, regardless of body weight

questions.text: What physical quality allows a player to accelerate quickly over short distances?
questions.explanation: Speed and power (explosive strength) allow a player to produce maximal force rapidly, enabling quick acceleration over short distances typical of sprints in football.

options.text: Explosive power and speed
High aerobic capacity
Flexibility
Core stability

questions.text: What is meant by 'cooling down' after a football match?
questions.explanation: A cool-down involves light activity and stretching after intense exercise to gradually lower heart rate, reduce muscle soreness, and begin the recovery process.

options.text: Light exercise and stretching to lower heart rate and begin recovery
Immediately sitting down and resting without any movement
Taking an ice bath for 30 minutes
Drinking cold water to lower core body temperature

questions.text: What is the approximate distance a top-level outfield footballer covers in a 90-minute match?
questions.explanation: Elite outfield players typically cover between 10 and 13 kilometres per match, combining walking, jogging, running, and sprinting.

options.text: 10–13 kilometres
3–5 kilometres
20–25 kilometres
6–8 kilometres

questions.text: What is 'muscle fatigue' in the context of a football match?
questions.explanation: Muscle fatigue occurs when the working muscles can no longer maintain the required force output, typically due to depletion of energy stores (glycogen) and accumulation of metabolic by-products.

options.text: The decline in muscle force output due to energy depletion and metabolic build-up
A muscle injury caused by a direct impact
The feeling of sleepiness after a long warm-up
Reduced flexibility caused by cold weather

questions.text: Why is sleep important for a football player's physical development?
questions.explanation: During sleep, the body releases growth hormone, repairs muscle tissue, consolidates motor learning, and restores energy stores — all critical for athletic development.

options.text: Sleep promotes muscle repair, hormone release, and motor learning consolidation
Sleep only matters for mental focus, not physical recovery
Sleep reduces muscle mass if done excessively
Sleep has no direct impact on physical performance

questions.text: What does 'resting heart rate' indicate about a player's fitness level?
questions.explanation: A lower resting heart rate typically indicates better cardiovascular efficiency — a well-trained heart pumps more blood per beat, so it does not need to beat as frequently at rest.

options.text: A lower resting heart rate indicates better cardiovascular fitness
A higher resting heart rate indicates better fitness
Resting heart rate is unrelated to fitness
A resting heart rate above 100 bpm is normal for athletes

questions.text: Which energy system is primarily used during a short, explosive sprint in football?
questions.explanation: Short explosive sprints (0–10 seconds) rely primarily on the phosphocreatine (ATP-PCr) system, which provides immediate energy without requiring oxygen.

options.text: The phosphocreatine (ATP-PCr) system
The aerobic oxidative system
The glycolytic lactic acid system
Fat oxidation
